title: Heaps and PriorityQueue in Java
summary: Min/max heap shape and order properties, heapify, PriorityQueue API, top-k, k-way merge, and median of a stream.

questions.prompt: A min-heap guarantees:
choices: The root is the smallest element
Each level is sorted left to right
questions.explanation: Only the root is guaranteed to be the global minimum. The rest of the heap is partially ordered.

questions.prompt: Java PriorityQueue default ordering:
questions.explanation: PriorityQueue<Integer> uses natural Comparable order — smallest element at the head.

questions.prompt: Output?
questions.explanation: Min-heap: poll() returns and removes the smallest element — 1.

questions.prompt: For top-K LARGEST elements, use:
choices: Max-heap of all n elements, poll k times
Min-heap of size k — evict minimum when size exceeds k
Both A and B are O(n log k) for large n
questions.explanation: Min-heap of size k: heap's root is the *smallest of the top-k*. New element replaces it if larger. Space O(k), time O(n log k).

questions.prompt: Building a heap from n elements using sift-down (heapify) takes:
questions.explanation: Sift-down from index n/2-1 down to 0. Most nodes are near leaves with short paths — total work sums to O(n).

questions.prompt: In a heap array, the parent of element at index i is at:
questions.explanation: Zero-indexed: parent(i) = (i-1)/2. Left child = 2i+1, right child = 2i+2.

questions.prompt: What is the parent index of element at index 5?
questions.explanation: (5 - 1) / 2 = 2 (integer division).

questions.prompt: peek() vs poll() on a PriorityQueue:
choices: peek() reads root in O(1) without removing; poll() removes root in O(log n)
questions.explanation: peek = look at the best without removing; poll = remove and restore heap order via sift-down.

questions.prompt: After poll() removes the root from a min-heap, heap order is restored by:
choices: Moving the last element to root and sifting it down
Swapping root with its smaller child repeatedly upward
questions.explanation: Sift-down: compare with children, swap with smaller child, repeat until heap property restored. O(log n).

questions.prompt: Heap sort time and space complexity:
choices: O(n log n) time, O(n) space
O(n log n) time, O(1) space
questions.explanation: Build heap O(n) + n extractions each O(log n) = O(n log n). In-place — O(1) extra space.

questions.prompt: Median of a data stream uses two heaps because:
choices: One heap is not enough memory
Max-heap holds lower half, min-heap holds upper half — their tops bracket the median in O(1)
Two heaps are faster than sorting
questions.explanation: Splitting the stream at the median lets you read it in O(1) without sorting all data.

questions.prompt: In the two-heap median approach, the size invariant to maintain is:
choices: Lower half size >= upper half size, differ by at most 1
Sizes can differ by any amount
questions.explanation: Keeping sizes within 1 ensures the median is either the top of the larger heap or average of both tops.

questions.prompt: K-way merge of k sorted lists with total n elements using a heap runs in:
questions.explanation: Each of the n elements is inserted and removed from a heap of size k — O(log k) per element.

questions.prompt: Which real problems map naturally to a min-heap?
choices: Checking if a graph is bipartite
questions.explanation: Graph bipartiteness uses BFS/DFS coloring, not a heap. The others need fast minimum extraction.

questions.prompt: Inserting into a heap (offer/add) works by:
choices: Adding to root and sifting down
Adding at the END and sifting UP to restore heap order
Binary search to find insertion position
questions.explanation: Append to end (preserves shape), then sift-up: swap with parent while smaller than parent. O(log n).

questions.prompt: Max-heap creation in Java:
questions.explanation: reverseOrder() makes largest element the root — peek() returns 7.

questions.prompt: To find the k-th largest element in an unsorted array optimally:
choices: Max-heap poll k times O(n + k log n)
Min-heap of size k O(n log k)
questions.explanation: O(n log k) min-heap is better than full sort when k << n. QuickSelect O(n) avg exists but heap is simpler.

questions.prompt: A complete binary tree with height h has at most how many nodes?
questions.explanation: Each level i has 2^i nodes; summing levels 0 to h gives 2^(h+1) - 1.

questions.prompt: Dijkstra's algorithm uses a min-heap to:
choices: Always process the unvisited node with the current shortest known distance
questions.explanation: Greedy: the min-heap ensures the next node processed has the definitively shortest path.

questions.prompt: Why can't we efficiently search for an arbitrary element in a heap?
choices: Heap order is weaker than BST order — only parent-child relationship is guaranteed, not left-right
It would take O(n log n)
Only the root can be accessed
questions.explanation: A heap only guarantees parent ≤ children; arbitrary search requires O(n) scan unlike a BST's O(log n).

flashcards.back: SHAPE: complete binary tree. ORDER: parent ≤ children (min) or parent ≥ children (max).
peek(): O(1) read root. poll(): O(log n) remove root + sift-down.
Use MIN-heap of size k. New element replaces heap min if larger. O(n log k).
Sift-down from index n/2-1 to 0. O(n) total — not O(n log n).
Max-heap (lower half) + min-heap (upper half). Balance sizes ±1. Median = tops.
Min-heap of k heads. poll → append to result → push next from same list. O(n log k).

project.brief:
Write HeapsLab.java with:
1. topKLargest(int[] nums, int k) → int[]  using a min-heap of size k
2. kthLargest(int[] nums, int k) → int  (same approach, return just the k-th)
3. mergeKSorted(int[][] lists) → List<Integer>  k-way merge
4. MedianFinder class with addNum(int) and findMedian() → double
Include main() tests: k=1, k=n, streams with even and odd counts.

checklist.text: topKLargest evicts heap min only when size exceeds k
mergeKSorted handles empty sublists without NullPointerException
findMedian returns a double (not int) — handles even-count averaging correctly

hints: topKLargest: after processing all elements, drain the min-heap into result — it comes out in ascending order.
mergeKSorted: store [value, listIdx, elemIdx] in heap so you know which list to advance.
MedianFinder: always push to lower first, then rebalance — this guarantees lower.max <= upper.min.
